unit.subject: English
unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 3. This unit explores the world of clothing, fashion, and sustainability. You will learn about how clothes are made, why the choices we make as consumers matter, and what people around the world are doing to protect the environment through sustainable fashion. You will also build your skills in multi-word verbs (phrasal verbs like "put on", "dress up" and "turn off") and learn how to use the passive voice to describe processes and actions. Alongside the topic work, you will read the story "A Voice in the Valley", about a quiet girl named Hannah in rural Ethiopia who discovers that her writing can inspire an entire community. By the end of the unit, you will write an eco-project report, give a short speech about sustainable fashion, and feel confident using passive structures and multi-word verbs in your own writing.
unit.learningPath: Preview the goals and vocabulary. Read and annotate the source texts. Compare evidence, viewpoint and source reliability. Apply the grammar focus. Draft and revise a cohesive multi-paragraph response. Present, defend, reflect and complete the quiz.

readings.title: Clothing, Fashion, and Sustainability: source text 1
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Welcome to Unit 3. This unit explores the world of clothing, fashion, and sustainability. You will learn about how clothes are made, why the choices we make as consumers matter, and what people around the world are doing to protect the environment through sustainable fashion. You will also build your skills in multi-word verbs (phrasal verbs like "put on", "dress up" and "turn off") and learn how to use the passive voice to describe processes and actions. Alongside the topic work, you will read the story "A Voice in the Valley", about a quiet girl named Hannah in rural Ethiopia who discovers that her writing can inspire an entire community. By the end of the unit, you will write an eco-project report, give a short speech about sustainable fashion, and feel confident using passive structures and multi-word verbs in your own writing.
By the end of this unit, you will be able to:
Use vocabulary related to clothing, materials, fast fashion, and the environment.
Understand and use multi-word verbs (phrasal verbs) such as put on, take off, dress up, throw away, and give away.
Form sentences in the present passive ("Cotton is grown in many countries") and past passive ("The dress was made by hand").
Combine modal verbs with the passive voice ("Clothes should be recycled", "Waste must be reduced").
Distinguish between strong obligation (must, have to) and weak obligation (should, might).
Add extra information to sentences using non-defining relative clauses ("My grandmother, who is a tailor, makes beautiful dresses").
Use literary vocabulary (simile, metaphor, imagery, personification) to analyse and improve your own writing.
Read and respond to texts about fashion and sustainability.
Write an eco-project report with clear structure and persuasive language.
Prepare and deliver a short speech about sustainable fashion choices.
Before you begin, look through these important words. You will meet them throughout the unit. Full definitions and practice activities are in your separate Vocabulary file.
These words relate to the unit theme of clothing, fashion, and the environment:
fast fashion -- cheap clothing produced quickly and in large quantities, often causing waste and pollution.
sustainable -- able to continue over time without harming people or the environment.
upcycle -- to take something old or unwanted and turn it into something new and useful.
textile -- any type of cloth or woven fabric, such as cotton, silk, or polyester.
garment -- a piece of clothing.
landfill -- a place where rubbish and waste are buried in the ground.
carbon footprint -- the total amount of greenhouse gases produced by our actions.
ethical -- acting in a way that is fair, honest, and does not harm others.
consumer -- a person who buys goods or services.
recycle -- to process used materials so they can be made into new products.
These words help you analyse stories and improve your writing:
imagery -- words that create pictures in your mind by describing things in vivid detail.
simile -- comparing two things using like or as ("Her words flowed like river water").
metaphor -- saying something is something else to show a similarity ("The moon is a mirror").
personification -- giving human qualities to non-human things ("The wind whispered secrets").
alliteration -- repeating the same sound at the beginning of several words ("Isaac Piper picked peppers").

readings.title: Clothing, Fashion, and Sustainability: source text 2
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
narrate -- to tell a story or describe events in order.
dialogue -- a conversation between two or more people in a story or play.
figurative -- using words in a way that is not literal, to create a special effect.
persuade -- to convince someone to do or believe something.
thesis -- a statement or main idea that someone wants to prove in their writing.
Reading: Fashion, Faith, and Our Planet
Read this passage carefully. It is about clothing choices, sustainability, and how young people can make a difference. Pay attention to the vocabulary words you have just learned.
The modern fashion industry is one of the biggest polluters on Earth. Every year, around one hundred billion garments are produced worldwide. Many of them are made from cheap synthetic textiles like polyester, which is produced from oil. These clothes are often worn only a few times before they are thrown away. In fact, the average person today buys sixty percent more clothing than people did just fifteen years ago, and keeps each item for about half as long. This cycle of buying, wearing briefly, and discarding is called fast fashion.
Fast fashion harms the environment in several ways. Growing cotton uses huge amounts of water: it takes about ten thousand litres to produce a single pair of jeans. Dyeing fabrics releases toxic chemicals into rivers and lakes. Transporting clothes from factories in one country to shops in another adds to carbon emissions. And when unwanted clothes end up in landfill, they can take hundreds of years to decompose, releasing harmful gases as they break down.
However, there is hope. Around the world, young people are finding creative solutions. Some organise clothing swaps, where friends and neighbours exchange garments they no longer wear instead of throwing them away. Others learn to upcycle old clothes: a worn-out shirt can be turned into a tote bag, and faded jeans can become a pencil case. In East Africa, tailors and seamstresses have a long tradition of repairing and reimagining garments, giving them a second life. Many Muslim families already practise modest and mindful consumption, choosing quality over quantity and passing clothes down through the family.
Schools are getting involved too. Some have started eco-fashion projects, where students design outfits from recycled materials and present them in a fashion show. Others write reports and give speeches to persuade their communities to buy less and choose better. These projects show that fashion can be both beautiful and sustainable.
The next time you get dressed, think about the story behind your clothes. Every garment has a journey -- from the field where the cotton was grown, to the factory where it was sewn, to the shop where it was sold. By choosing carefully, repairing what we have, and sharing what we no longer need, we can all help protect the planet we share. As the saying goes, the most sustainable garment is the one already in your wardrobe.
Answer these questions in full sentences:
What is fast fashion? Explain it in your own words.
Name three ways that fast fashion harms the environment, according to the passage.
What is upcycling? Give one example from the passage and one of your own.
Find these words in the passage and explain what each one means:

readings.title: A Voice in the Valley, part 1
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
The sun rose slowly over the green ridges of the valley, painting golden light on the rooftops of houses made of clay and straw. In the village of Selota, people woke with the rhythm of roosters, the smell of injera cooking, and the soft chanting of morning prayers.
At the heart of this small village lived Hannah, a quiet girl with a loud imagination. Her name meant "lemon," but her thoughts were sweet and strong, like spiced tea. Hannah kept a red notebook under her bed, where she loved to narrate stories, write poems, and paraphrase tales she had heard from her grandmother.
Though her voice was soft, her words were bold. In her stories, trees could walk, rivers could whisper, and the sun signified hope. Her writing was full of personification, similes, and metaphors, which made even the cows seem like scholars and the mountains like sleeping giants.
But no one else ever read her notebook.
“You should share your writing,” her older brother Isaac often said. “People would be amazed.”
Hannah would shake her head. “No. They’ll laugh. They’ll say it’s childish.”
Isaac made an assertion that stuck in her mind: “A voice kept silent is like a drum never played.”
At school, Hannah was invisible. She was not the student who won races or spoke in assemblies. But in literature class, when their teacher asked them to find imagery in a poem, Hannah’s hand always went up.
“Imagery,” she said once, “is not just about what you see. It’s what you feel. The sound of a lonely flute can make your heart shiver.”
Her teacher, Miss Nadia, smiled. “That’s quite an inference.”
One Thursday afternoon, Miss Nadia brought a surprise. “We are launching a school journal. Anyone may submit a story, poem, or article. The best ones will be published.”
That night, she sat under the mosquito net with her red notebook. Her hands trembled as she tore out two pages. Her story was about a boy who could hear the stars speak. She had used alliteration, rhyme, and a strong voice.
“You must submit this,” Isaac said when he read it. “It should be shared, Hannah.”
She hesitated, then dropped the story into the submission box the next morning.
Then one Monday in July, a poster appeared on the school wall. The journal had been printed. It was titled Echoes of the Highlands. Hannah’s eyes widened when she saw her name under a story titled “Star Listener.”
Beneath the title was a short caption: “A tale where silence speaks louder than words.”
Students crowded around the journal. Some read aloud. Others laughed, clapped, or nodded. A boy pointed at Hannah and said, “This is yours?”
“Your words are powerful,” said Sophia, a girl from another class. “How did you write like that?”
Hannah smiled. “I just listened. And I wrote.”
Her story became the talk of the week. Even the school principal, Mr. Ishmael — who, until then, rarely spoke to students — approached her and said, “You have a gift.”
That week, Miss Nadia asked Hannah to help her lead a creative writing session.
“You’ll teach students how to use figurative language,” she said. “You can explain repetition, rhythm, and rhyme. Oh — and the connotation of words too.”

readings.title: A Voice in the Valley, part 2
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Hannah was nervous. But she agreed.
She prepared her lesson carefully. She used coloured chalk to write examples on the blackboard. She explained that metaphors, like “The moon is a mirror,” make writing richer. She asked, “What does ‘The wind danced’ mean?”
A hand shot up. “It’s personification!”
During her lesson, she used examples from her story. “Good writers emphasize emotion. They use dialogue to make readers feel connected. A well-placed simile, like ‘as brave as a lion’, helps readers imagine better.”
She also showed students how to infer meaning from a sentence and how to structure their stories in chronological order.
Later that day, Isaac teased her. “Now you’re the teacher!”
“Only a little,” she laughed. “But it feels good.”
News of her teaching spread. Hannah was invited to speak at a youth arts event in the town nearby. She almost said no.
“I can’t. What if I fail?”
“You might fail,” Isaac said. “But your story might be remembered, too. That’s a risk worth taking.”
At the event, she read a new poem filled with rhythm, rhyme, and heart. Her words flowed like river water. The crowd was silent, listening.
One judge stood and made an assertion: “This poem must be shared with schools across the region.”
The next month, it was. Hannah’s poem was printed in three school newsletters.
When reporters visited her school, Hannah was asked, “How did you start?”
She said, “With a notebook I hid under my bed.”
Hannah’s success inspired others. Her classmate Haile, who had never shared a poem before, wrote a piece full of figurative beauty. Sophia began a comic strip series. Even shy Bilen submitted an essay about her grandfather’s garden.
Miss Nadia told the class, “Creativity should be encouraged, not hidden. And sometimes, a story must be told so that others can find the courage to tell theirs.”
The writing club, which had once been empty, now had over twenty students.
At the end of the term, a ceremony was held at the community hall. Hannah was called to the stage to receive a certificate of excellence. A banner behind her read: “The Pen Paints What the Voice Feels.”
As she held the certificate, she thought of all the students who once feared to speak.
“This,” she said softly, “is not just for me. It’s for every quiet voice that dares to speak.”
In the weeks that followed, the journal grew. More pages, more stories, more young writers who felt brave enough to write. Hannah began helping to edit the submissions, teaching others about punctuation and tone. “Even punctuation,” she told one younger student, “can shape how a story is perceived.”
She also began writing a new story titled “Voices in the Dust,” a tale that characterised a group of children from different regions who overcame silence with art. Her new story included strong exposition, expressive dialogue, and a clear thesis: that art can heal what fear breaks.
Hannah reflected on how language could change minds. Words could persuade gently or impose unfairly, depending on how they were used. And in her stories, she chose to persuade — to offer kindness, strength, and truth.
By the following year, Selota School had become known as the “Valley of Voices,” a place where young writers were born. One of Hannah’s favourite moments came when a younger girl handed her a poem titled “My Grandmother’s Hands,” filled with repetition and loving imagery.

grammar.title: Multi-Word Verbs
grammar.explanation:
What the rule means and why it matters
Multi-word verbs (also called phrasal verbs) are special combinations of a main verb and one or more particles (small words like on, off, up, down, away, out). Together, these words create a new meaning that is often different from the meaning of the verb alone. For example, "put" means to place something, but "put on" means to wear something. Multi-word verbs are extremely common in everyday English, and knowing them will help you communicate more naturally.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
What the rule means and why it matters
Multi-word verbs (also called phrasal verbs) are special combinations of a main verb and one or more particles (small words like on, off, up, down, away, out). Together, these words create a new meaning that is often different from the meaning of the verb alone. For example, "put" means to place something, but "put on" means to wear something. Multi-word verbs are extremely common in everyday English, and knowing them will help you communicate more naturally.
How to form and use multi-word verbs
Identify the main verb: The first part is usually a common verb (put, take, give, throw, turn, look, carry).
Add a particle: The second part is a preposition or adverb (on, off, up, down, away, out, after) that changes the verb's meaning.
Combine them: The verb and particle together form the multi-word verb.
Use in a sentence: Place the multi-word verb where a normal verb would go.
Examples with clothing and sustainability
put on = to wear: "I will put on my warm coat before going outside."
take off = to remove: "Please take off your shoes at the door."
dress up = to wear smart or special clothes: "We like to dress up for Eid."
throw away = to discard: "Do not throw away clothes that can be mended."
give away = to give for free: "We give away clothes we have outgrown."
try on = to test if clothes fit: "She tried on three scarves before choosing one."
turn off = to switch off: "Turn off the sewing machine when you finish."
carry out = to do or complete: "The students carried out a survey on fast fashion."
Separable and inseparable multi-word verbs
Some multi-word verbs are separable -- you can put the object between the verb and the particle:
"I put my jacket on." or "I put on my jacket." (both correct)
"She threw the old dress away." or "She threw away the old dress." (both correct)
When the object is a pronoun (it, them, her, him), you must separate:
"I put it on." (correct)   NOT "I put on it." (incorrect)
"She threw them away." (correct)   NOT "She threw away them." (incorrect)
Some multi-word verbs are inseparable -- the verb and particle must stay together:
"She looks after her little brother." (correct)   NOT "She looks her little brother after." (incorrect)
"We came across an old sewing machine in the attic." (correct)
Common mistakes and how to fix them
Mistake: Using the wrong particle. "Put off your shoes" means to delay, not to remove!
Fix: "Take off your shoes" is correct for removing them.
Mistake: Forgetting irregular past forms. "I putted on my jacket."
Fix: "put" does not change: "I put on my jacket" (past tense is the same).
Memory tip
Think of multi-word verbs as a team: the verb is the player and the particle is the coach that changes the game plan. "Put" alone is ordinary, but "put on", "put off", "put up with" -- each coach gives it a totally different strategy!
Practice 1
Choose the correct multi-word verb to complete each sentence:
I need to ______ my sweater because it is cold outside. (put on / take off)
Can you ______ the radio? It is too loud! (turn up / turn off)
We will ______ for the school ceremony tonight! (dress down / dress up)
Please do not ______ that plastic bottle. Recycle it instead. (throw away / give away)
She ______ the dress in the shop, but it did not fit. (tried on / put on)
We ______ our old school uniforms to younger students every year. (give away / throw away)
grammar.commonMistake: Check meaning, sentence structure and punctuation before accepting an answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply multi-word verbs in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

grammar.title: Present Passive
grammar.explanation:
What the rule means and why it matters
In English, we usually talk about who does an action: "Farmers grow cotton." But sometimes we want to focus on what happens to the object rather than who does it. The present passive lets us do this: "Cotton is grown in many countries." This is especially useful when we do not know who did the action, when the doer is not important, or when we want to focus on the process or result.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
What the rule means and why it matters
In English, we usually talk about who does an action: "Farmers grow cotton." But sometimes we want to focus on what happens to the object rather than who does it. The present passive lets us do this: "Cotton is grown in many countries." This is especially useful when we do not know who did the action, when the doer is not important, or when we want to focus on the process or result.
How to form the present passive
The structure is: subject + is / are + past participle.
Identify the object of the active sentence: "Workers sew the uniforms." (Object: the uniforms)
Move the object to the front to become the new subject: "The uniforms..."
Use is (singular) or are (plural) + the past participle of the main verb: "The uniforms are sewn."
Add 'by' + doer (optional): "The uniforms are sewn by workers."
Examples
"Cotton is grown in Egypt, India, and the United States."
"Clothes are made in factories around the world."
"Water is used in huge amounts to dye fabrics."
"Old garments are collected and sorted for recycling."
"The school uniform is designed by a local tailor."
Common mistakes and how to fix them
Mistake: "The books are write."
Fix: Use the past participle: "The books are written."
Mistake: "The cake is bake by my mother."
Fix: "The cake is baked by my mother."
Memory tip
Think of the present passive as asking: "What happens to the object?" If you can answer that question, you are on the right track!
Practice 2
Fill in the blanks with the correct present passive form of the verb in brackets:
The letter __________ (send) by the postman every morning.
These dresses __________ (make) from organic cotton.
The school uniforms __________ (design) by a local company.
Plastic bottles __________ (collect) for recycling every Friday.
The fabric __________ (dye) using natural colours from plants.
grammar.commonMistake: Check meaning, sentence structure and punctuation before accepting an answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply present passive in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

grammar.title: Modal Verbs + Passive Voice
grammar.explanation:
What the rule means and why it matters
Modal verbs (can, could, must, should, will, might, may) express possibility, ability, permission, obligation, or advice. When we combine them with the passive voice, we can talk about what should, must, or can be done to something -- without naming who does it. This is very useful for rules, recommendations, and formal writing.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
What the rule means and why it matters
Modal verbs (can, could, must, should, will, might, may) express possibility, ability, permission, obligation, or advice. When we combine them with the passive voice, we can talk about what should, must, or can be done to something -- without naming who does it. This is very useful for rules, recommendations, and formal writing.
How to form it
The structure is: modal verb + be + past participle.
"Old clothes should be recycled, not thrown away."
"Waste must be reduced to protect the environment."
"Natural fabrics can be composted at the end of their life."
"This book may be borrowed from the library."
"New designs will be created from recycled materials."
"Mistakes might be corrected later."
Common mistakes and how to fix them
Mistake: Incorrect word order. "Should cited be the book."
Fix: modal + be + past participle: "The book should be cited."
Mistake: Forgetting the past participle. "The homework should be do."
Fix: "The homework should be done."
Mistake: Mixing active and passive. "You should write the report" is active. The passive version is: "The report should be written."
Memory tip
Remember M-B-P: Modal + Be + Past participle. This simple formula will keep your sentences in the right order every time!
Practice 3
Complete each sentence using the correct modal + passive structure:
The project ______ (should / finish) by next week.
This song ______ (can / sing) at the school concert.
The instructions ______ (must / follow) carefully.
The old uniforms ______ (could / donate) to charity.
More trees ______ (should / plant) around the school.
grammar.commonMistake: Check meaning, sentence structure and punctuation before accepting an answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply modal verbs + passive voice in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

grammar.title: Strong and Weak Obligation
grammar.explanation:
What the rule means and why it matters
In English, we use different words to express how necessary something is. Understanding the difference between strong obligation (you have no choice) and weak obligation (it is a good idea, but you can choose) helps you communicate clearly about rules, advice, and expectations.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
What the rule means and why it matters
In English, we use different words to express how necessary something is. Understanding the difference between strong obligation (you have no choice) and weak obligation (it is a good idea, but you can choose) helps you communicate clearly about rules, advice, and expectations.
Strong obligation
Use must or have to when something is required or there is no choice:
must (the speaker feels it is necessary): "You must wear a seatbelt in the car."
have to (an external rule requires it): "Students have to wear school uniform every day."
"I have to finish my eco-project report by Friday."
"We must reduce plastic waste to protect the oceans."
Weak obligation
Use should or might when something is recommended or suggested, but not required:
should (advice or recommendation): "You should try to buy fewer new clothes."
might (a gentle suggestion or possibility): "You might want to repair that tear instead of buying a new shirt."
"We should reuse bags instead of throwing them away."
"You might enjoy the upcycling workshop after school."
Key differences
must comes from the speaker's own feeling of necessity.
have to often comes from an outside rule, law, or authority.
should is for advice -- it is a good idea, but you can choose.
might is the gentlest -- it suggests a possibility.
Common mistakes and how to fix them
Mistake: Using "should" for a strict rule: "You should wear a seatbelt" (too weak for a safety rule).
Fix: "You must wear a seatbelt." (strong obligation)
Mistake: Confusing "must" and "have to": "I must go to the dentist" (if it is an appointment, not your personal feeling).
Fix: "I have to go to the dentist." (external requirement)
Memory tip
Think of must and have to as hard rules -- like walls you cannot walk through. Think of should and might as soft guides -- like signposts that point the way but let you choose your path.
Practice 4
Complete each sentence with must, have to, should, or might:
You ______ eat your vegetables to stay healthy. (weak obligation)
I ______ finish my project by Friday! (strong obligation -- external deadline)
You ______ wear your coat; it is freezing outside! (strong obligation -- personal feeling)
We ______ visit the recycling centre this weekend. (gentle suggestion)
Students ______ hand in their homework on time. (school rule)
You ______ try upcycling old clothes -- it is fun! (advice)

grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply strong and weak obligation in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

grammar.title: Non-Defining Relative Clauses
grammar.explanation:
What the rule means and why it matters
A non-defining relative clause adds extra information about a noun. It does not change the main meaning of the sentence -- it just gives us more detail. Non-defining relative clauses are separated from the rest of the sentence by commas, and they use who (for people) or which (for things). They make your writing more interesting and informative.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
What the rule means and why it matters
A non-defining relative clause adds extra information about a noun. It does not change the main meaning of the sentence -- it just gives us more detail. Non-defining relative clauses are separated from the rest of the sentence by commas, and they use who (for people) or which (for things). They make your writing more interesting and informative.
How to form non-defining relative clauses
Identify the noun you want to add information about: "My grandmother makes beautiful dresses."
Think of the extra detail: "She is a tailor."
Use who (people) or which (things) to connect: "My grandmother, who is a tailor, makes beautiful dresses."
Place commas before and after the clause.
Examples
"My grandmother, who is a tailor, makes beautiful dresses." (who = people)
"Cotton, which needs a lot of water to grow, is one of the most common fabrics." (which = things)
"The Eco Club, which meets every Thursday, organised a clothing swap." (which = things)
"Mr. Ishmael, who is the school principal, praised Hannah's writing." (who = people)
"Polyester, which is made from oil, takes hundreds of years to decompose." (which = things)
Important rules
Always use commas before and after the clause.
Do NOT use "that" in non-defining relative clauses. Use who or which only.
The sentence must make sense even if you remove the relative clause.
Test: "My grandmother makes beautiful dresses." -- still makes sense without the clause. The clause ("who is a tailor") is extra, not essential.
Common mistakes and how to fix them
Mistake: Forgetting commas. "My brother who is very talented loves football."
Fix: Add commas: "My brother, who is very talented, loves football."
Mistake: Using "that" instead of "who" or "which". "The car, that is red, belongs to my mum."
Fix: "The car, which is red, belongs to my mum."
Memory tip
Think of non-defining relative clauses as bonus gifts of information. They are like sprinkles on a cupcake -- the cupcake is complete without them, but sprinkles make it extra special! Always wrap them in commas, and use who for people and which for things.
Practice 5
Combine each pair of sentences using a non-defining relative clause. Remember to add commas!
My best friend is very creative. She loves to draw.
The school library has many books. It was built last year.
Mr. Ibrahim teaches us English. He is from Djibouti.
Fast fashion produces a lot of waste. It is a growing global problem.
Hannah wrote poems in her red notebook. She lived in a small highland village.

grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.

writing.title: Writing 1: Eco-Project Report
writing.promptAndInstructions:
In this section you will plan, draft, and write a short eco-project report. Imagine your school has started a sustainability project related to clothing. You will write a report about the project for your school newsletter or website.
Step 1: Choose your project
Pick one of these project ideas, or think of your own:
A clothing swap event at school, where students exchange clothes they no longer wear.
An upcycling workshop, where old clothes are turned into new items like bags, cushion covers, or headbands.
A poster campaign to persuade students and families to buy less fast fashion.
A fashion show using outfits made entirely from recycled or upcycled materials.
Step 2: Plan your report
Use this structure:
Title: Give your report a clear, interesting title.
Introduction: What is the project? Why did your school start it? (2-3 sentences)
What we did: Describe the main activities. Use past passive where possible ("Posters were designed...", "Old clothes were collected..."). (4-5 sentences)

quizzes.question: What does 'alliteration' mean?
quizzes.options: A strong, regular, repeated pattern of sound or movement. | Doing or saying something again and again. | The marks used in writing to separate sentences and clarify meaning, like full stops, commas, and question marks. | The repetition of the same sound at the beginning of several words in a sentence or phrase.
quizzes.correctAnswer: The repetition of the same sound at the beginning of several words in a sentence or phrase.
quizzes.explanation: "Isaac Piper picked a peck of pickled peppers" is a famous example of alliteration.

quizzes.question: What does 'rhyme' mean?
quizzes.options: A word that has the same ending sound as another word. | Doing or saying something again and again. | The marks used in writing to separate sentences and clarify meaning, like full stops, commas, and question marks. | Using words in a way that is not literal, often to create a special meaning or effect.
quizzes.correctAnswer: A word that has the same ending sound as another word.
quizzes.explanation: In the poem, "cat" and "hat" are examples of words that rhyme.

quizzes.question: What does 'rhythm' mean?
quizzes.options: A strong, regular, repeated pattern of sound or movement. | A way of comparing two things using the words "like" or "as". | The marks used in writing to separate sentences and clarify meaning, like full stops, commas, and question marks. | Using words in a way that is not literal, often to create a special meaning or effect.
quizzes.correctAnswer: A strong, regular, repeated pattern of sound or movement.
quizzes.explanation: The drummer kept a steady rhythm that made everyone want to dance.

quizzes.question: What does 'repetition' mean?
quizzes.options: A way of comparing two things using the words "like" or "as". | A way of describing something by saying it is something else, to show a similarity. | Doing or saying something again and again. | Using words in a way that is not literal, often to create a special meaning or effect.
quizzes.correctAnswer: Doing or saying something again and again.
quizzes.explanation: In class, we practise our vocabulary through repetition to remember the words better.

quizzes.question: What does 'punctuation' mean?
quizzes.options: A way of comparing two things using the words "like" or "as". | A way of describing something by saying it is something else, to show a similarity. | A way of describing something non-human by giving it human qualities. | The marks used in writing to separate sentences and clarify meaning, like full stops, commas, and question marks.
quizzes.correctAnswer: The marks used in writing to separate sentences and clarify meaning, like full stops, commas, and question marks.
quizzes.explanation: Using punctuation correctly helps make your writing clear and easy to understand.

quizzes.question: What does 'figurative' mean?
quizzes.options: A way of describing something by saying it is something else, to show a similarity. | A way of describing something non-human by giving it human qualities. | Using words in a way that is not literal, often to create a special meaning or effect. | Words that create pictures in your mind by describing things in vivid detail.
quizzes.correctAnswer: Using words in a way that is not literal, often to create a special meaning or effect.
quizzes.explanation: When she said the classroom was a zoo, she was using a figurative expression to show it was very noisy.

quizzes.question: What does 'simile' mean?
quizzes.options: A way of comparing two things using the words "like" or "as". | A way of describing something non-human by giving it human qualities. | The feelings or ideas that a word makes you think of, beyond its basic meaning. | Words that create pictures in your mind by describing things in vivid detail.
quizzes.correctAnswer: A way of comparing two things using the words "like" or "as".
quizzes.explanation: "Her smile was as bright as the sun" is a simile.

quizzes.question: What does 'metaphor' mean?
quizzes.options: A way of describing something by saying it is something else, to show a similarity. | The feelings or ideas that a word makes you think of, beyond its basic meaning. | To show or represent something. | Words that create pictures in your mind by describing things in vivid detail.
quizzes.correctAnswer: A way of describing something by saying it is something else, to show a similarity.
quizzes.explanation: In the sentence "Time is a thief," the word "thief" is a metaphor.

quizzes.question: What does 'personification' mean?
quizzes.options: A way of describing something non-human by giving it human qualities. | The feelings or ideas that a word makes you think of, beyond its basic meaning. | To show or represent something. | To tell a story or describe events in order.
quizzes.correctAnswer: A way of describing something non-human by giving it human qualities.
quizzes.explanation: "The wind whispered secrets to the trees" is an example of personification.

quizzes.question: What does 'imagery' mean?
quizzes.options: To say something in a different way, using different words. | To show or represent something. | To tell a story or describe events in order. | Words that create pictures in your mind by describing things in vivid detail.
quizzes.correctAnswer: Words that create pictures in your mind by describing things in vivid detail.
quizzes.explanation: The poet used beautiful imagery to help us imagine the sunset over the valley.
